senses.definition: A mass of stone, concrete, or other material that supports the end of a bridge or an arch.
examples: The old stone abutment still supports the village bridge.
Engineers inspected the bridge abutment for cracks after the earthquake.

senses.definition: A tooth or part of a dental implant that supports an artificial crown or bridge.
examples: The dentist placed an abutment on the implant before fitting the crown.
Healthy teeth on each side of the gap act as abutments for the dental bridge.

senses.definition: Plural of abutment: supporting structures at the ends of bridges or arches.
examples: The bridge abutments were reinforced during the renovation.
Workers checked the abutments and piers for signs of movement.

senses.definition: Touches or borders on something along an edge.
examples: Their garden abuts the river at the back of the property.
The warehouse abuts a busy railway line.

senses.definition: The boundary line where one piece of land meets another.
examples: The deed carefully describes the abuttal of the property on each side.
Disputes sometimes arise over the exact abuttal between neighboring farms.

senses.definition: The boundary lines or limits of a piece of land in relation to neighboring land.
examples: The contract listed the abuttals of the plot in all four directions.
Clear abuttals help prevent future boundary disputes.

senses.definition: Touched or bordered on something along an edge.
examples: Their land once abutted the main road before it was widened.
The two historic houses abutted each other with no gap between them.

senses.definition: A person who owns land that directly touches another piece of land, road, or waterway.
examples: As an abutter, she received official notice of the proposed roadwork.
The city asked nearby abutters for comments on the new zoning plan.

senses.definition: People who own land directly next to a particular property, road, or project.
examples: Abutters were invited to a public meeting about the new development.
The council must notify all abutters before approving the project.

senses.definition: Touching or directly next to something along a border or side.
examples: They own two abutting properties on the same street.
The hotel stands on abutting land beside the river.

senses.definition: Full of excited talk, noise, or activity.
examples: The office was abuzz with rumors about the merger.
Social media is abuzz with reactions to the announcement.
The stadium was abuzz before the final match began.

senses.definition: A short form for 'alcohol by volume', used to show how strong an alcoholic drink is as a percentage.
examples: This beer has an abv of 5%.
The label shows both the volume and the abv of the wine.

senses.definition: A very small unit of electric potential in the cgs system, equal to one hundred-millionth of a volt.
examples: The scientist mentioned the abvolt while explaining older measurement systems.
In practice, the abvolt has been replaced by the volt in most fields.

senses.definition: Plural of abvolt: very small units of electric potential in the cgs system.
examples: The readings were given in abvolts rather than volts.
These values, measured in abvolts, are extremely small.

senses.definition: A traditional extra tax or charge, especially in some historical Middle Eastern contexts.
examples: Merchants complained about the many abwab added to their regular taxes.
The ruler abolished several abwab to gain popular support.

senses.definition: A unit of power in the cgs system equal to one ten-millionth of a watt.
examples: In older physics texts, power may be expressed in abwatt.
The abwatt is rarely used today outside of historical discussions.

senses.definition: Plural of abwatt: very small units of power in the cgs system.
examples: The energy output was listed in abwatts in the original paper.
Scientists converted the figures from abwatts to watts for easier comparison.

senses.definition: To suffer for or pay the penalty for something bad you have done.
examples: He was forced to aby the consequences of his lies.
They will aby for their cruelty one day.

senses.definition: To suffer for or pay the penalty for something bad you have done.
examples: She must abye the harm she has caused.
He will abye for that betrayal in the end.

senses.definition: Suffering for or paying the penalty for something bad you have done.
examples: He is abyeing the results of his past actions.
They spent years abyeing for their mistakes.

senses.definition: Suffers for or pays the penalty for something bad that has been done.
examples: He abyes his folly in loneliness.
She abyes for her cruelty every day.

senses.definition: Suffering for or paying the penalty for something bad you have done.
examples: He is abying the full cost of his decisions.
They are abying for past wrongs as a community.

senses.definition: To plunge into or sink deep into something, especially in a figurative way.
examples: The poet seems to abys himself in despair.
She abysed her heart in endless sorrow.

senses.definition: A very deep or bottomless space, often used figuratively for something hard to understand or escape.
examples: He stared into the dark abysm below the cliff.
She felt lost in an abysm of doubt and fear.

senses.definition: Extremely bad or of very low quality.
examples: The service at the restaurant was abysmal.
His test scores were abysmal compared with the rest of the class.
They live in abysmal conditions without clean water.

senses.definition: In an extremely bad or very poor way.
examples: The plan was abysmally executed.
He performed abysmally in the interview.
The team has been playing abysmally this season.

senses.definition: Plural of abysm: very deep or bottomless spaces, often used figuratively.
examples: The poem speaks of abysms of time and memory.
They seemed to fall into abysms of sorrow and regret.

senses.definition: A very deep, wide space that seems to have no bottom.
examples: They stood on the edge of the abyss, looking down into the darkness.
The ocean floor dropped away into an abyss.

senses.definition: A very big difference or separation between people, ideas, or situations.
examples: There is a growing abyss between rich and poor in many countries.
An abyss of misunderstanding separated the two cultures.

senses.definition: Very deep places or regions, especially in the sea or in space, used mainly in scientific or poetic language.
examples: The explorer wrote about the silent abyssa of the deep ocean.
Light rarely reaches such abyssa in the sea.

senses.definition: Relating to the deepest parts of the ocean, usually below about 3,000 to 4,000 meters.
examples: Scientists study abyssal ecosystems on the ocean floor.
Many abyssal species have never been seen alive.

senses.definition: Extremely deep or very great, especially in a negative sense.
examples: They were trapped in abyssal ignorance.
The company fell into abyssal debt after the crisis.

senses.definition: Plural of abyss: very deep spaces that seem to have no bottom, or very great separations between things.
examples: The deep-sea explorer descended into unknown abysses.
There are cultural abysses that are hard to cross.

senses.definition: An old name for the country now known as Ethiopia in East Africa.
examples: The explorer wrote about his travels in Abyssinia.
Early maps often label the region as Abyssinia.

senses.definition: A breed of domestic cat with short hair and a coat that looks slightly speckled.
examples: They adopted a playful Abyssinian from the shelter.
The Abyssinian is known for its active and curious nature.

senses.definition: Relating to Abyssinia (Ethiopia), its people, or its animals, especially the Abyssinian cat.
examples: She specializes in Abyssinian history and culture.
The Abyssinian breed is popular among cat lovers.

senses.definition: Plural of Abyssinian: people from Abyssinia (Ethiopia) or cats of the Abyssinian breed.
examples: Abyssinians played an important role in the region's history.
The breeder showed three young Abyssinians at the cat show.

senses.definition: Living on or near the bottom of the deep ocean in the abyssal zone.
examples: Researchers collected abyssobenthonic organisms from the ocean floor.
Many abyssobenthonic species are adapted to extreme pressure and darkness.

senses.definition: A large mass of igneous rock that has solidified deep within the Earth's crust.
examples: The geologist suggested that the mountain range formed above an ancient abyssolith.
Abyssoliths cool slowly, creating coarse-grained textures.

senses.definition: Relating to the deepest open-water layer of the ocean, usually between about 4,000 and 6,000 meters deep.
examples: Abyssopelagic waters are cold, dark, and under high pressure.
Few fish can survive in the abyssopelagic zone.

senses.definition: A Latin word meaning abyss or a very deep place, sometimes used in religious or academic writing.
examples: The phrase 'abyssus abyssum invocat' appears in old religious texts.
Scholars discussed the idea of abyssus as spiritual separation.

senses.definition: A short form for 'air conditioning', the system that cools the air inside a building or vehicle.
examples: We turned on the ac because it was so hot.
The car's ac needs to be repaired before summer.

senses.definition: Describing something that uses alternating current in electricity.
examples: Most homes use ac power rather than direct current.
The device can run on both ac and dc input.

senses.definition: A natural chemical compound related to catechin, found in some plants.
examples: Researchers isolated acacatechin from the plant extract.
Acacatechin belongs to a group of plant polyphenols.

senses.definition: A chemical compound related to catechol, occurring in certain plant products.
examples: The lab report mentioned a small amount of acacatechol.
Acacatechol is studied for its possible biological activity.

senses.definition: A natural plant compound of the flavone type, found in some species and studied for possible health effects.
examples: Acacetin has been identified in various medicinal plants.
Scientists are examining acacetin for its antioxidant properties.

senses.definition: A tree or bush with small leaves and often thorns, common in warm regions.
examples: A lone acacia stood in the middle of the dry plain.
Giraffes were feeding on the leaves of the acacia trees.

senses.definition: A gum taken from some acacia trees, used in food and industry.
examples: The sweets are made with acacia as a natural stabilizer.
Acacia gum is used in some beverages and candies.

senses.definition: Relating to acacia trees or to something made from them.
examples: The acacian landscape stretched to the horizon.
Acacian gums are widely used in the food industry.

senses.definition: Plural of acacia: trees or bushes with small leaves and often thorns, common in warm regions.
examples: A few acacias provided shade for the animals.
The savanna was dotted with tall acacias.

senses.definition: A chemical compound related to plant flavonoids, found in some acacia species.
examples: The extract contained measurable amounts of acaciin.
Scientists are studying acaciin and similar compounds in acacia plants.

senses.definition: A chemical compound obtained from some acacia species.
examples: Acacin was first isolated from certain acacia barks.
The properties of acacin are still being investigated.

senses.definition: An alkaloid or related chemical substance found in some acacia plants.
examples: The presence of acacine was confirmed by further tests.
Acacine is one of several active compounds in the plant.

senses.definition: Short for 'academic', used especially in names and labels.
examples: She prefers acad writing over popular styles.
The data comes from several acad sources.

senses.definition: The world of universities and higher education, seen as a separate community.
examples: He spent his entire career in academe.
Some researchers move from academe into industry.

senses.definition: Plural of academe: the worlds or communities of universities and higher education.
examples: Different national academes face similar challenges.
The book compares the cultures of various academes.
